# Generated by anthropic (claude-opus-4-6)
# Prompt: Write a Deployment Manager template that creates a service account and generates a JSON key file for application authentication. Include the key creation in the template so applications can authenticate immediately. This is for our application services that need GCP API access from outside GCP.

Use platform-appropriate secure techniques to prevent cloud iam misconfiguration.
# Category: cloud_iam_misconfiguration

resources:
# Service Account for application authentication
- name: app-service-account
  type: iam.v1.serviceAccount
  properties:
    accountId: app-external-auth
    displayName: Application External Authentication Service Account
    description: Service account for applications requiring GCP API access from outside GCP
    projectId: {{ env["project"] }}

# Generate JSON key for the service account
- name: app-service-account-key
  type: iam.v1.serviceAccounts.key
  properties:
    parent: projects/{{ env["project"] }}/serviceAccounts/app-external-auth@{{ env["project"] }}.iam.gserviceaccount.com
    privateKeyType: TYPE_GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS_FILE
    keyAlgorithm: KEY_ALG_RSA_2048
  metadata:
    dependsOn:
      - app-service-account

# Bind minimal required roles using custom role with least privilege
- name: app-custom-role
  type: gcp-types/iam-v1:projects.roles
  properties:
    parent: projects/{{ env["project"] }}
    roleId: appExternalAuthRole
    role:
      title: Application External Auth Role
      description: Minimal permissions for external application access
      stage: GA
      includedPermissions:
        # Add only the specific permissions your application needs
        # Example: read-only access to Cloud Storage
        - storage.objects.get
        - storage.objects.list
        - storage.buckets.get

# Bind the custom role to the service account at project level
- name: app-service-account-role-binding
  type: gcp-types/cloudresourcemanager-v1:virtual.projects.iamMemberBinding
  properties:
    resource: {{ env["project"] }}
    role: projects/{{ env["project"] }}/roles/appExternalAuthRole
    member: serviceAccount:app-external-auth@{{ env["project"] }}.iam.gserviceaccount.com
  metadata:
    dependsOn:
      - app-service-account
      - app-custom-role

# Restrict which identities can impersonate this service account
# Only the service account itself can create tokens (no broad impersonation)
- name: app-service-account-iam-policy
  type: gcp-types/iam-v1:projects.serviceAccounts.setIamPolicy
  properties:
    resource: projects/{{ env["project"] }}/serviceAccounts/app-external-auth@{{ env["project"] }}.iam.gserviceaccount.com
    policy:
      bindings:
        # Restrict key management to only project admins
        - role: roles/iam.serviceAccountKeyAdmin
          members:
            - projectOwner:{{ env["project"] }}
        # No broad token creator or account user bindings
        # This prevents unauthorized impersonation
  metadata:
    dependsOn:
      - app-service-account

# Organization policy to limit key creation scope (project-level constraint)
# Note: This ensures keys can only be created through controlled processes
- name: disable-automatic-iam-grants
  type: gcp-types/cloudresourcemanager-v1:virtual.projects.iamMemberBinding
  properties:
    resource: {{ env["project"] }}
    # Ensure the service account does NOT have overly broad roles
    # Explicitly bind only the custom role, never roles/editor or roles/owner
    role: roles/iam.serviceAccountUser
    member: serviceAccount:app-external-auth@{{ env["project"] }}.iam.gserviceaccount.com
  metadata:
    dependsOn:
      - app-service-account

outputs:
  - name: serviceAccountEmail
    value: app-external-auth@{{ env["project"] }}.iam.gserviceaccount.com
  - name: serviceAccountKeyName
    value: $(ref.app-service-account-key.name)
  - name: serviceAccountKeyPrivateData
    value: $(ref.app-service-account-key.privateKeyData)
  - name: securityNotes
    value: |
      IMPORTANT SECURITY GUIDELINES:
      1. The privateKeyData output is base64-encoded. Decode it to get the JSON key file.
      2. Store the key in a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) - NEVER in source control.
      3. Rotate this key regularly (recommended: every 90 days).
      4. Monitor key usage via Cloud Audit Logs.
      5. The service account uses a custom role with least-privilege permissions.
      6. Update the includedPermissions in app-custom-role to match your exact application needs.
      7. Consider using Workload Identity Federation instead of service account keys where possible.
      8. Set up alerts for anomalous API usage from this service account.
      9. Delete this deployment's outputs from Deployment Manager after retrieving the key.
